title: Generating Fock state exceeding 10000 excitations with near unit fidelity by adaptive generalized-parity measurement
abstract: Macroscopic Fock states provide valuable resources for quantum information processing and quantum metrology. We here propose an adaptive generalized-parity-measurement protocol to create macroscopic Fock states with more than $10000$ excitations. For a general system with a discrete spectrum, e.g., a bosonic mode, that is coupled to an ancillary qubit, we derive a construction rule of either a diagonal generalized parity measurement (GPM) or a displaced GPM with intervals adaptive to the last outcome of repeated measurements on the qubit. Different from the probabilistic protocols based on postselection, in which only a single prescribed sequence of free-evolution-measurement is survived, our protocol retains every measurement trajectory by converting the outcome randomness of the ancillary-qubit measurement to the adaptive update of GPM. Using the resonant Jaynes-Cummings (JC) model, our protocol can transform a large coherent state to a large Fock state of photon numbers up to $n_t=\mathcal{O}(10^4)$ within $10$ rounds of measurements, where the averaged fidelity reaches about $80\%$. The probability for obtaining such a large Fock state with a fidelity above $99\%$ remains about $35\%$ with respect to the ensemble sampling. Our protocol also applies to displaced thermal states, indicating its robustness against a moderate thermal mixture.
summary: This paper details an adaptive generalized-parity-measurement protocol designed to create macroscopic Fock states exceeding 10,000 excitations in a bosonic system modeled by the JC Hamiltonian. By adaptively updating measurement intervals based on qubit outcomes, the protocol avoids the low success probabilities of traditional postselection methods. It demonstrates high-fidelity state generation, proving its utility for quantum information processing in cavity QED settings.
main_problem: The primary goal is to generate macroscopic Fock states containing over 10,000 excitations with high fidelity.
main_result: The proposed adaptive generalized-parity-measurement protocol successfully achieves this state preparation in about 10 rounds, demonstrating high fidelity and robustness.
method: The protocol uses repeated, adaptively timed projective measurements on an ancillary qubit coupled to a bosonic mode, effectively implementing a generalized parity measurement.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using the resonant Jaynes-Cummings (JC) model, involving a bosonic mode (like a cavity field) coupled to an ancillary qubit. The initial state can be a coherent or displaced thermal state.
key_observables: Fock state number ($n_t$), fidelity ($ ar{F}_N$), and success probability for achieving high fidelity.
important_parameters: Number of excitations ($n_t = \mathcal{O}(10^4)$), number of measurement rounds ($\sim 10$), and the initial state parameters (e.g., temperature $ eta$).
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes the coupling allows for the derivation of a general rule for the adaptive GPM, and approximations are used in the resonant regime ($\Delta=0$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the adaptive measurement circuit, the population distribution evolution in Fock space over several rounds, and the convergence of the averaged fidelity and the probability distribution of the final fidelity for both pure and thermal states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem, details the adaptive GPM protocol derived from the JC model, provides theoretical derivations for the measurement operators (Kraus operators), and concludes with numerical results demonstrating high-fidelity state preparation for large photon numbers.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses state preparation in cavity QED systems, which is a cornerstone of modern AMO physics and quantum optics. The adaptive measurement scheme offers a powerful, non-postselection alternative to generating complex quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Circuit diagram about constructing the adaptive gen- eralized parity measurement by repeatedly performing pro- jective measurements on the ancillary qubit with outcomes mk = e or g. The intervals τk between measurements depend on the last measurement record. After N rounds of mea- surements, the induced operation on the main system ρc is equivalent to a GPM M(N) GP [a displaced GPM T (N) GP ] if the ﬁnal outcome is mN = e (mN = g).

Figure 1 is the diagram of the our protocol, in which the duration of free evolution is adaptively updated ac- cording to the last measurement-outcome record. To ef- ﬁciently describe GPM on the main system, we set a GPM label nt(k) (an integer) to specify the center Fock state of the generalized-parity subspace after k rounds of measurement. k = 0 denotes the initial GPM label. The index of the ﬁnal obtained Fock state is between nt(0) − p

FIG. 2. An example about the variation of the population distribution in Fock space after the ﬁrst 4 rounds for a spe- ciﬁc sequence of measurement results {|g⟩, |e⟩, |g⟩, |g⟩}. The initial GPM label is set as nt(0) = 1000. The red bar in each panel marks the updated GPM label nt(N) for measurements outcome mN = e or nt(N) + 1 for mN = g due to Eq. (16).

Figure 3 demonstrates the performance of our adaptive protocol for the large Fock-state generation under various initial coherent states |α⟩with |α|2 = nt(0). The numer- ical simulation is performed over 500 ≤nt(0) ≤20000. This range is mainly limited by our computer memory (we merely use a desktop with an Intel Core i7-7700 pro- cessor with 3.60 gigahertz in frequency and 16 gigabyte in memory) not by the protocol itself, which is irrelevant to the excitation number of Fock state. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the ﬁdelity averaged over all measurement trajectories can rapidly converge to about 80% in less than 10 rounds of measurement, which is con- sistent with the logarithmic scaling behavior...
